topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle, yellowed paper felt alien in my hands, a relic dredged up from the dusty confines of an old desk drawer. It was my father’s handwriting, looping and precise, yet the content chilled me to the bone. A resignation letter. Decades ago. I unfolded the stiff parchment, the edges crumbling slightly under the pressure of my fingers. "Effective immediately," the script declared, "I tender my resignation from all prior obligations." A tight knot formed in my chest, a familiar, acidic burn rising in my throat. I reread the formal language, the detached professionalism, and felt a wave of profound disappointment wash over me. He had simply walked away, neatly and without fanfare. It wasn't a dramatic exit; it was just... done. I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound a small, sharp snap in the quiet room. What a waste of potential, he thought, as I watched the paper distort in my fist.

[story 2]

I stared at the document under the harsh glare of the desk lamp, my jaw tight. The ink seemed faded, the paper thin as tissue. This was the official record of my parent’s departure, a neat little footnote in the sprawling narrative of our family history. Reading the words—a formal notice of leaving—I let out a short, humorless puff of air through my nose. It was so utterly unremarkable. All that striving, all the unspoken ambition that had fueled his early years, reduced to this sterile declaration. I traced a line with my fingertip, feeling a distinct hardening in my posture. There was no anger bubbling up, just a deep, cold settling in my stomach. He hadn't fought for his place; he had just packed his bags and left. The sheer lack of resistance was what truly grated on me.

[story 3]

My breath hitched as I unfolded the letter. The texture of the aged paper was rough against my palms, a tangible connection to a past I barely understood. My eyes scanned the neat block text, landing on the final, definitive statement. My lips pressed into a thin, straight line. It was a performance, this nostalgia, pretending this faded document held some profound secret or tragedy. It didn't. It was just an administrative act. A quiet surrender. A flicker of something sharp and unpleasant crossed my face—a tightening around the eyes that I quickly smoothed away. I folded the paper with deliberate, precise movements, tucking it into a box as if sealing away something utterly inconsequential and entirely uninspiring. He had simply given up. And I felt nothing but a distinct sense of dismissal toward that quiet capitulation.
